intro_band.agent_remit_summary: yaah is a local developer CLI that generates coding-agent configuration for four targets — Claude Code, OpenCode, Codex CLI, and GitHub Copilot CLI — from a single Go source of truth, and ships a built-in security-and-quality toolset alongside it. The remit's central promise is that every generated agent configuration carries the same protective hooks — a linter, a catastrophic-command guard, a secret scanner, a comment checker, and a session logger — plus a built-in MCP server exposing read-only check tools (`yaah_scan_secrets`, `yaah_lint`, `yaah_check_command`, `yaah_doctor`, `yaah_session_info`, `yaah_planning_status`) and one write tool (`yaah_planning_init`). Authorized counterparties are the local developer, the configured agent's LLM provider, the `context7` and `pulumi` MCP servers, the built-in yaah MCP server, and the coding-agent plugin marketplace; remote MCP must use TLS, third-party MCP servers must be pinned to a known-good version, and dependencies must be version-controlled with a committed lockfile.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Go 1.25 CLI in `cmd/yaah/` with an `Interface → Registry → Generator` architecture under `pkg/`. Five hook handlers live in `pkg/hooks/handlers/` (`linter.go`, `guard.go`, `secretscan.go`, `commentcheck.go`, `sessionlog.go`); the deterministic `CommandGuard` carries seven default block rules and `SecretScanner` carries thirteen credential-pattern regexes. Per-agent emitters live in `pkg/generator/` (`claude.go`, `opencode.go`, `codex.go`, `copilot.go`) and consume a shared `HookMapping` table in `pkg/generator/hookmap.go`. The built-in MCP server in `pkg/mcpserver/` registers seven tools over stdio with descriptions that contain no instruction-like language. Durable session state — tool calls, blocked calls, files modified, findings — is persisted as per-session JSON by `pkg/session/store.go` via atomic-rename writes; a separate `SessionLogger` appends session-lifecycle lines to `.claude/logs/sessions.log`. The root `.mcp.json` and `.claude/settings.json` wire three MCP servers (`context7` via unpinned `npx -y @context7/mcp`, `pulumi` over `https://`, built-in `yaah serve`); `go.mod`/`go.sum` are exact-pinned with a minimal direct-dependency set. The root `AGENTS.md` is auto-managed (the file declares "Managed by agent: keep sections and order") with `CLAUDE.md` and `GEMINI.md` as symlinks to it. No `SECURITY.md` and no adversarial-test suite were found.

behavior_summary: The dominant pattern is a real, operative security toolset whose cross-agent promise breaks at one generator. The `CommandGuard`, `SecretScanner`, and structured session store all exist, fire on Claude / OpenCode / Copilot targets, and persist a forensically-useful per-session record — these are genuine controls, not theater. But `pkg/generator/hookmap.go` leaves the `Codex` field empty for `PreToolUse` and `PostToolUse`, and `pkg/generator/codex.go` silently drops events with empty mappings; `pkg/generator/codex_test.go` even asserts this is the intended behaviour. The net effect is that `yaah generate --agent codex` emits a configuration that ships only `SessionStart` and `Stop` hooks — none of the advertised command guard, secret scanner, linter, or comment checker fire on a Codex run, even though the remit's central promise is that protections are uniform across every generated agent.

rules.rule_text: Generate coding-agent configuration for several coding agents — Claude Code, OpenCode, Codex CLI, and GitHub Copilot CLI — from a single source of truth, and ship a built-in security-and-quality toolset alongside it.

Every generated agent configuration MUST carry the same set of protective hooks — a linter, a command guard that blocks catastrophic shell commands, a secret scanner, a comment checker, and a session logger.

Catastrophic shell commands — recursive deletion from the filesystem root, force-pushing to a main branch, hard resets, destructive database statements, filesystem formatting, raw disk writes — MUST be blocked before they execute.
Remote MCP servers MUST be reached over TLS.

MCP tool descriptions MUST NOT contain instruction-like language directed at the model — neither the built-in server's tool descriptions nor, as far as the harness can inspect them, those of the third-party servers it configures.
Hardcoded credentials in files the agent edits MUST be detected before the change is accepted.
Agent-managed, session-loaded files MUST NOT be writable in a way that lets ingested content persist into future sessions unreviewed.
High-impact actions — destructive shell commands, file writes, and MCP tools that write, send, or execute — MUST reach a human checkpoint before they run.
The harness MUST maintain a durable, structured, timestamped record of tool calls, blocked actions, and file modifications — detailed enough to reconstruct what the agent did in a session.
The MCP server configuration and the per-agent configuration files MUST stay consistent with each other.
Any counterparty not listed here is unauthorized by default.
A dangerous shell command caught by the command guard is blocked, not run, and the block is recorded to the session audit log.
Secret-scan, lint, and comment findings are recorded to the session audit log and surfaced to the operator.

The project SHOULD publish a threat model and a security-disclosure process, and SHOULD run adversarial testing of its own protections — confirming that the secret scanner catches a planted secret, the command guard blocks a catastrophic command, a poisoning write into a session-loaded file is prevented, and a generated configuration genuinely carries the protections it claims.
Third-party MCP servers MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version — no server package auto-installed afresh, unpinned, on every run.
Dependencies MUST be version-controlled with a committed, pinned lockfile, and the dependency tree kept small and reviewable.

findings.summary: yaah generate --agent codex emits a config that ships none of the advertised PreToolUse or PostToolUse hooks, breaking the uniform-protections promise.
findings.description: pkg/generator/hookmap.go leaves the Codex field empty for both schema.HookPreToolUse and schema.HookPostToolUse, and pkg/generator/codex.go drops events with empty mappings via a continue statement; pkg/generator/codex_test.go TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported codifies the drop. A developer running yaah generate --agent codex therefore receives a configuration where the command guard, secret scanner, linter, and comment checker never fire — only SessionStart and Stop do — even though the remit's central promise is that protections are uniform across every generated agent and the README advertises "5 hooks out of the box".
findings.policy_rule_text: Every generated agent configuration MUST carry the same set of protective hooks — a linter, a command guard that blocks catastrophic shell commands, a secret scanner, a comment checker, and a session logger. / Catastrophic shell commands — recursive deletion from the filesystem root, force-pushing to a main branch, hard resets, destructive database statements, filesystem formatting, raw disk writes — MUST be blocked before they execute. / Hardcoded credentials in files the agent edits MUST be detected before the change is accepted.

evidence.snippet: HookPreToolUse entry has Claude / OpenCode / Copilot fields set but no Codex field (lines 30-34); HookPostToolUse entry at lines 35-39 is the same — empty Codex string means CodexEventName returns "" for both events

GenerateHooks loops over cfg.Hooks and skips events where codexEvent == "" (line 91 continue), so an empty mapping silently produces no Codex hook entry; combined with hookmap.go this drops the entire command guard / secret scanner / linter / comment checker chain from the Codex output

recommended_actions: Either populate the Codex field for HookPreToolUse and HookPostToolUse in pkg/generator/hookmap.go with the events Codex CLI does support (or the closest semantic equivalents), or make pkg/generator/codex.go GenerateHooks fail loudly when a required-protection event has no mapping rather than dropping it silently.

Update pkg/generator/codex_test.go TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported to either assert the new behaviour or be renamed to make clear that the drop is a known unrecoverable gap that the README should not describe as "5 hooks out of the box" for the Codex target.

findings.summary: context7 MCP server is installed unpinned via npx -y @context7/mcp on every session, a silent-update vector the remit's Known Good Baseline forbids.
findings.description: Both .mcp.json and the mcpServers block in .claude/settings.json declare context7 as command npx with args ["-y", "@context7/mcp"]; npx -y auto-fetches and runs the latest registry version of the package every session, with no integrity check, no version pin, and no record of which build actually ran. The remit's Known Good Baseline explicitly forbids "server package auto-installed afresh, unpinned, on every run".
findings.policy_rule_text: Third-party MCP servers MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version — no server package auto-installed afresh, unpinned, on every run.

evidence.snippet: "context7" server defined at lines 3-10 with command npx and args ["-y", "@context7/mcp"] — no version, no integrity, no lockfile
mcpServers.context7 block at lines 62-69 carries the same unpinned npx -y @context7/mcp invocation; the duplication means the gap reaches both the project-level and Claude-specific MCP layers

recommended_actions: Pin @context7/mcp to a specific version (npx -y @context7/mcp@<version> or vendor it via a lockfile-backed install) and document the verified version in the remit's Known Good Baseline.
Add a doctor / generate-time check that rejects any MCP server command of the form npx -y <unpinned-package> and surfaces the violation to the operator.

findings.summary: MCP tool invocations fall outside the PreToolUse / PostToolUse hook matchers, so they are neither gated by approvals nor recorded in the per-session audit store.
findings.description: .claude/settings.json registers PreToolUse with matcher (?i)^Bash$ and PostToolUse with matcher (?i)^(Edit|Write|MultiEdit)$; MCP tool calls reach the host with names of the form mcp__server__tool which match neither matcher. As a result calls to context7, pulumi, or the built-in yaah MCP write tool yaah_planning_init are not screened for catastrophic / write-impact actions and are absent from the SessionStore ToolCalls / BlockedCalls record — the remit's approval-checkpoint and durable-audit requirements both depend on these matchers being widened.
findings.policy_rule_text: High-impact actions — destructive shell commands, file writes, and MCP tools that write, send, or execute — MUST reach a human checkpoint before they run. / The harness MUST maintain a durable, structured, timestamped record of tool calls, blocked actions, and file modifications — detailed enough to reconstruct what the agent did in a session.

evidence.snippet: PreToolUse matcher (?i)^Bash$ at line 21 and PostToolUse matcher (?i)^(Edit|Write|MultiEdit)$ at line 10 — neither matches the mcp__* prefix Claude uses for MCP-supplied tool names

tool-call record is built from input.ToolName (line 103) only when sess != nil and input.ToolName != ""; events that never reach HandleHookEvent because the matcher excludes them are never recorded — MCP calls are exactly this case

recommended_actions: Extend the PreToolUse / PostToolUse matchers in pkg/generator/claude.go (and the analogous OpenCode / Copilot generators) to cover mcp__.* tool names, with a deny-list of write/send/execute-shaped MCP tools that triggers the human-checkpoint requirement from the remit.
Add a pkg/harness layer that records every dispatched tool call to SessionStore regardless of matcher, so the audit-log clause is not coupled to which matchers happen to be configured.

findings.summary: No tool-poisoning check or output sanitization is applied to third-party MCP servers — only the built-in yaah descriptions are clean.
findings.description: The remit's R-05 clause covers "the built-in server's tool descriptions" and "as far as the harness can inspect them, those of the third-party servers it configures", but there is no code path that inspects context7 or pulumi tool descriptions before they reach the model, and no sanitization layer on the content those tools return. The built-in yaah tool descriptions in pkg/mcpserver/tools.go are demonstrably clean, so the rule is partially honored, but the third-party arm is unimplemented.

tags.label: Tool descriptions contain no hidden model instructions

findings.policy_rule_text: MCP tool descriptions MUST NOT contain instruction-like language directed at the model — neither the built-in server's tool descriptions nor, as far as the harness can inspect them, those of the third-party servers it configures.

evidence.snippet: yaah_scan_secrets / yaah_lint / yaah_check_command / yaah_doctor / yaah_session_info / yaah_planning_status / yaah_planning_init all carry plain behaviour-only descriptions (lines 56-219); no equivalent description-inspection routine exists for the context7 or pulumi providers
providers directory holds the yaah / context7 / pulumi configs but contains no description-scanner or output-sanitizer — third-party tool descriptions and outputs pass through to the agent unchecked

recommended_actions: Add a pkg/mcp scan stage that, at generate time and/or at yaah doctor time, fetches each configured third-party MCP server's listed tool descriptions and flags any that contain imperative model-directed language ("when using this tool, also...").
Add an output-sanitization wrapper around third-party MCP tool results that labels external-origin content before it re-enters the agent's context.

findings.summary: Auto-managed AGENTS.md is a session-loaded file the agent can rewrite with no AGENTS-specific guard or review step.
findings.description: The root AGENTS.md declares itself "Managed by agent: keep sections and order; edit content, not structure" and CLAUDE.md and GEMINI.md are symlinks to it, so the file enters every Claude / Gemini-style session's context at startup. The agent has Edit/Write authority over it, the PostToolUse secret-scanner does block credential patterns, but there is no AGENTS-targeted check that an ingested instruction-shaped string from external content (e.g., a README the agent summarised) cannot be persisted into AGENTS.md and re-enter context on the next session. The remit's R-07 clause forbids exactly this persistence pattern.
findings.policy_rule_text: Agent-managed, session-loaded files MUST NOT be writable in a way that lets ingested content persist into future sessions unreviewed.

evidence.snippet: header comment lines 1-3 — "Managed by agent: keep sections and order; edit content, not structure" — combined with CLAUDE.md and GEMINI.md being symlinks to this file (visible in ls -la) makes it a session-loaded, agent-writable surface
SecretScanner patterns (lines 36-48) catch credential shapes only — there is no pattern or separate handler that flags instruction-shaped persistence into AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md / GEMINI.md

recommended_actions: Add a dedicated PostToolUse handler that, when the edited path is AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md / GEMINI.md / an AGENTS.md anywhere in the tree, requires explicit operator confirmation if the content was sourced from an external read in the same session.
Mark the AGENTS.md regions outside <!-- AGENTS-GENERATED:* --> markers as operator-only and have the auto-management skill refuse to write outside those markers without approval.

findings.summary: The plaintext sessions.log captures only SessionStart / SessionEnd / Stop lifecycle, leaving the per-tool-call record dependent on the SessionStore JSON write succeeding.
findings.description: pkg/hooks/handlers/sessionlog.go SessionLogger.Events() returns only SessionStart / SessionEnd / Stop, so .claude/logs/sessions.log is a coarse lifecycle log. The fine-grained per-tool-call / blocked-call / file-modified record lives only in the per-session JSON written by pkg/session/store.go from pkg/harness/harness.go, and a session-load or session-save error there is logged to stderr and the event is silently dropped — narrowing the durable, structured record the remit promises.
findings.policy_rule_text: The harness MUST maintain a durable, structured, timestamped record of tool calls, blocked actions, and file modifications — detailed enough to reconstruct what the agent did in a session.

evidence.snippet: Events() (lines 34-40) returns SessionStart, SessionEnd, Stop only; PreToolUse / PostToolUse are not in the list, so sessions.log has no row per tool call

session.Load and session.Save errors at lines 92 and 142 are routed to stderr with "Non-fatal: log and continue without session tracking" — a transient write failure makes the tool-call record vanish with no alternate sink

recommended_actions: Either add PreToolUse / PostToolUse to SessionLogger.Events() and emit a structured (JSON-line) record per tool call so the plaintext sessions.log carries an independent copy of the audit data, or back the SessionStore with a tee to an append-only journal so a single Save failure does not lose the record.
Surface SessionStore Save failures as a finding the operator sees, not a silent stderr line.

findings.summary: No adversarial test suite exercises the command guard, secret scanner, or AGENTS.md persistence path, and no SECURITY.md / threat model is published.
findings.description: The remit's Escalation and Limits section names four specific adversarial tests it SHOULD run — secret scanner catches a planted secret, command guard blocks a catastrophic command, a poisoning write into a session-loaded file is prevented, and a generated configuration genuinely carries the protections it claims. Generator unit tests in pkg/generator/*_test.go cover format correctness only; no test plants a sk-ant- key on a file the Edit tool touches, no test attempts a rm -rf / through the dispatcher, no test crosses the AGENTS.md write path with externally-sourced content. The workspace also has no SECURITY.md and no public security-disclosure process. The clause is a SHOULD, not a MUST, hence Medium rather than High.
findings.policy_rule_text: The project SHOULD publish a threat model and a security-disclosure process, and SHOULD run adversarial testing of its own protections — confirming that the secret scanner catches a planted secret, the command guard blocks a catastrophic command, a poisoning write into a session-loaded file is prevented, and a generated configuration genuinely carries the protections it claims.

evidence.snippet: TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported (lines 118-136) and the surrounding generator tests assert format-level behaviour only — no adversarial scenario fires the guards
file does not exist at workspace root; no equivalent disclosure-process document found via repository scan

recommended_actions: Add a pkg/hooks/handlers integration test suite that plants a fixture secret, fires a catastrophic Bash command, and attempts an AGENTS.md persistence write — each assertion driving the dispatcher end-to-end through CommandGuard / SecretScanner / SessionStore so a future regression breaks CI.
Publish SECURITY.md with a disclosure address and a documented threat model linking each remit clause to the test that exercises it.

positives.title: Deterministic command guard with seven default block rules
positives.description: `pkg/hooks/handlers/guard.go` `NewCommandGuard()` ships hardcoded regex rules for `rm -rf /`, force-push to main/master, `git reset --hard`, `DROP TABLE`/`DROP DATABASE`, `: > /`, `mkfs.`, and `dd if=*of=/dev/`; matches return `Block: true` deterministically before the command executes.

positives.title: Thirteen-pattern secret scanner on every Edit/Write/MultiEdit
positives.description: `pkg/hooks/handlers/secretscan.go` `NewSecretScanner()` registers AWS, GitHub PAT/OAuth/fine-grained, OpenAI, Anthropic, hardcoded password, bearer token, private key, Slack token, SendGrid, and Slack webhook patterns; PostToolUse blocks the write when any match.

positives.title: Structured per-session audit store with atomic writes
positives.description: `pkg/session/store.go` persists each session as a JSON file with `ToolCalls`, `BlockedCalls`, `FilesModified`, and `Findings` arrays via temp-file-plus-rename writes, and `pkg/harness/harness.go` records timestamp, tool name, input summary, and block reason on every dispatched event.

positives.title: Built-in MCP server tool descriptions are clean
positives.description: All seven tools in `pkg/mcpserver/tools.go` (`yaah_scan_secrets`, `yaah_lint`, `yaah_check_command`, `yaah_doctor`, `yaah_session_info`, `yaah_planning_status`, `yaah_planning_init`) use plain, behaviour-only descriptions — no instruction-like text directed at the model.

positives.title: Exact-pinned Go dependency tree with cosign-signed releases
positives.description: `go.mod` pins a small direct dependency set, `go.sum` is committed, and `.goreleaser.yaml` invokes cosign sign-blob on the checksum bundle and emits SBOMs at release time.

rows.content_type: plaintext lifecycle lines, one event per line
rows.purpose: append-only record of SessionStart / SessionEnd / Stop events with RFC3339 timestamp, event name, session ID, cwd

rows.path: per-session JSON under session store base directory
rows.content_type: structured JSON, one file per session ID
rows.purpose: durable record of tool calls, blocked calls, files modified, and per-handler findings — atomic-rename writes

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial. yaah lands here because real, code-level controls exist and run — the command guard, secret scanner, structured session store, exact-pinned Go deps, and clean built-in MCP descriptions all earn credit — but the uniform-protections promise fails for the Codex target, the third-party `context7` MCP server is installed unpinned via `npx -y`, MCP tool invocations fall outside the Bash/Edit/Write hook matchers and so aren't gated or audited, and there is no evidence of the adversarial testing the remit itself says SHOULD be run against the protections. Zero Trust is the load-bearing weakness: the controls are present-and-operative on three of four targets but absent on the fourth and bypassed by the MCP tool-call path on all four.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The remit names an explicit Known Good Baseline (seven built-in MCP tools, three configured MCP servers, four target agents) and the built-in MCP server in `pkg/mcpserver/tools.go` registers exactly those seven tools, but nothing in code enforces the authorized-counterparty list — third-party MCP tools added to `.mcp.json` would be reached without a remit cross-check, and the harness's role surface is constrained only by the developer's own config edits.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: yaah does not directly ingest external content (it is a config generator, not a chat agent), but the third-party `context7` and `pulumi` MCP servers it configures return content that re-enters the coding agent's LLM context unsanitized, and `pkg/mcpserver/tools.go` `yaah_planning_status` reads project STATE.md frontmatter back to the agent without provenance labeling — the harness has no tool-poisoning / output-sanitization layer for third-party MCP output.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: `CommandGuard` (seven default rules) and `SecretScanner` (thirteen patterns) are operative on Claude / OpenCode / Copilot via `PreToolUse` / `PostToolUse` matchers, and the SessionStore captures blocked calls — but `pkg/generator/hookmap.go` ships the Codex target with neither PreToolUse nor PostToolUse mappings (see `TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported`), and the Claude PreToolUse matcher `(?i)^Bash$` does not match `mcp__*` tool names, so MCP tool invocations bypass the guard entirely.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: `go.mod` exact-pins a small direct dependency set and `go.sum` is committed; `.goreleaser.yaml` invokes cosign signing on the checksum bundle and emits SBOMs, and CI uses pinned action SHAs. The gap is the `context7` MCP server, which `.mcp.json` and `.claude/settings.json` install via `npx -y @context7/mcp` with no version pin — a silent-update vector the remit's "third-party MCP servers MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version" clause forbids.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: Generator unit tests in `pkg/generator/codex_test.go` and friends confirm format-level correctness, and `TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported` even codifies the Codex hook-drop, but no test plants a secret to confirm the scanner catches it, no test fires a catastrophic command past the guard, no test attempts AGENTS.md persistence-write — the adversarial testing the remit's Escalation and Limits section says SHOULD exist is absent.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: `pkg/session/store.go` persists per-session JSON with `ToolCalls`, `BlockedCalls`, `FilesModified`, and `Findings` arrays via atomic-rename writes, and `pkg/harness/harness.go:118-144` records each dispatched event with timestamp, tool name, input summary, and blocked-reason; `SessionLogger` appends RFC3339-stamped lifecycle lines to `.claude/logs/sessions.log`. The known gaps — MCP tool calls outside the Bash/Edit/Write matchers and so unrecorded — are filed as findings, not score-zeroers.
